title: Compliance and Supply Chain Monitor
gist: A TechInsights product page for an export-control and supply-chain compliance service that maps chip reverse-engineering results to specific ECCNs and tracks BIS-listed entities. Mostly promotional, but it names the concrete regulatory hooks (ECCNs 3A090, 4A003, 5A002; FDPR; CCL Categories 3/4/5) most relevant to AI-accelerator and memory export risk.

insights.claim: Reverse-engineering results for advanced chips can be mapped directly to specific U.S. Export Control Classification Numbers, notably 3A090, 4A003, and 5A002, which gate AI accelerators, FPGAs, and high-performance memory.
insights.speaker: TechInsights
insights.so_what: These ECCNs are the operative chokepoints for whether a given AI/memory chip can ship to restricted destinations; watch them as the binary gate on China-exposed semiconductor revenue.
insights.quote: TechInsights maps reverse engineering results directly to key ECCNs, including 3A090, 4A003, and 5A002.
insights.locator: Automated ECCN Mapping & Export Risk Flagging

insights.claim: The Foreign Direct Product Rule (FDPR) and deemed-export rules extend U.S. export-control reach to chips and dual-use items classified under CCL Categories 3, 4, and 5, even where U.S.-origin IP or tooling is only one input.
insights.speaker: TechInsights
insights.so_what: U.S.-origin IP/tooling dependencies create export exposure even for foreign-made parts; a useful lens for assessing which non-U.S. chipmakers are still constrained by U.S. rules.
insights.quote: The platform assesses FDPR applicability, identifies deemed export risks, and highlights dual-use classifications under CCL Categories 3, 4, and 5.
insights.locator: Automated ECCN Mapping & Export Risk Flagging

insights.claim: The October 2022 and 2023 U.S. chip restrictions are treated as the reference baseline for semiconductor export-control policy, against which new BIS rule changes are tracked.
insights.speaker: TechInsights
insights.so_what: The Oct 2022/2023 rules remain the policy anchor; incremental BIS updates should be read as deltas from that baseline when sizing China-revenue risk.
insights.quote: a historical database of major policy changes such as the U.S. chip restrictions from October 2022/2023

insights.claim: Teardown-derived bills of materials can trace design wins, fab sourcing, and country of origin to assess market share and trade risk at the company, country, and industry level.
insights.speaker: TechInsights
insights.so_what: BOM-level fab and origin tracing can surface hidden supply-chain dependencies before they show up in earnings or sanctions actions.

insights.claim: Dynamic profiles of BIS-listed and high-risk entities link demand-side end-use markets to supply-side dependencies such as specific fabs, chip designers, and source countries.
insights.speaker: TechInsights
insights.so_what: Mapping a flagged entity's fab and designer dependencies helps anticipate which suppliers are exposed if that entity is sanctioned.
